imageCaption: A sprawling hand-drawn diagram fills two open notebook pages: overlapping circles representing emotional resonance points, connected by spiral arrows that curve backward as well as forward through time. Sticky notes cluster around margin spaces. One note reads: 'What anchors the anchor?' in blue pen.
text:
The Barcelona Metro was supposed to be a functional piece of public transportation. It was currently doing something significantly more complicated, which was existing as a map that showed you where you'd already been, where you were about to go, and several destinations that hadn't been invented yet.

Aaliyah stood at the Plaça Reial station trying to decide which version of the platform was the actual one and which ones were temporal suggestions. In one timeline, the metro line curved north toward Montjuïc. In another, it spiraled inward toward the city center. In a third, it had apparently decided to run backward through the Sagrada Familia district, which suggested the universe had opinions about their itinerary.

"This is helpful," she muttered to no one in particular, "if terrifying."

"Three short barks," Elijah reported. He was standing next to Sully, translating the dog's assessment with the confidence of someone who'd become fluent in temporal anxiety. "Minor glitch. Established pattern. Also Sully says we should take the northern line."

"Why?" Anthony asked. He was holding his phone up, presumably trying to photograph the layered metro system, which was probably useless since photography seemed to make glitches either crystallize or multiply depending on the weather. "The northern line goes to Montjuïc. We want to head south toward the airport."

"Sully doesn't explain his reasoning," Elijah said patiently. "He just barks and we've learned that he's usually correct about the implications of temporal disturbance, so we go with it."

This was accurate. Sully had not yet been wrong about glitch locations, severity, or directional preference, despite having the cognitive capacity of a moderately enthusiastic golden retriever. Somewhere in the logic of this new version of reality, dogs had become more reliable than GPS.

Luisana was somewhere deeper in the station with her journal open, interviewing station workers about when exactly the metro had started showing them multiple timeline options. She would emerge with field notes that somehow made the impossible sound like a normal commuting inconvenience. This was her particular gift.

"If Sully says north," Aaliyah said, "we go north. But we're taking the solid-looking one. Not the theoretical timeline one."

She pulled out her diagram notebook as they descended the platform stairs—the ones that were definitely real, or at least more real than the ones that seemed to suggest the station had once been carved into the side of a building that didn't exist anymore. Her sketches from the cathedral were still fresh in her mind. The concept of an anchor. The understanding that if they wanted to heal time, they first had to know what they were healing toward.

The metro car that arrived was the kind that looked halfway between solid and suggestion. Aaliyah found this increasingly normal and increasingly horrifying.

As they settled into seats, her mother emerged from the depths of the station with her notebook and the expression of someone who'd learned something crucial and was annoyed at herself for not figuring it out sooner.

"The metro system is redundant," Luisana said, not bothering with preamble. "It has been for years. They've been building new lines, optimizing routes, trying to anticipate demand. But the first iteration—the original metro from the 1920s—it's still there underneath everything. The city built a new system on top of the old system without removing the old one."

"And?" Aaliyah prompted, because her mother had that particular tone that meant a conclusion was waiting for permission to be spoken aloud.

"And the glitch is following the old routes," Luisana continued, settling into the seat next to her daughter. "The temporal fractures are appearing in the places where the city's navigation system is most confused about what it's supposed to be. The system can't decide if it's the 1920s metro or the 2020s metro, so it's showing you both."

Aaliyah's diagram notebook was suddenly very important. She started drawing quickly—the metro as two systems overlapping, the old route and the new route occupying the same physical space while refusing to decide which one was real. Then she drew her family: five people and a dog, moving through a system that was fundamentally confused about which version of itself should exist.

"We're doing this," she said slowly. "The family is doing exactly this. We're the new route and the old route at the same time. We're moving forward toward Curaçao and backward through the timeline that got us here. We're trying to exist in two incompatible systems simultaneously and the universe is breaking because of it."

"Yes," Luisana said. "And that's not a problem you're solving. That's a feature."

The metro car lurched. Not physically—the motion was smooth—but temporally. Aaliyah felt the sensation of moving through time and against time at the same time. Like being in a car that was driving forward while also being driven backward. Like the momentum of the journey contained its own contradiction.

"ARIA-7 said the anchor is what doesn't move," Aaliyah said. "But what if the anchor isn't something static? What if it's something that can hold two versions of itself at the same time? What if it's something that's supposed to be paradoxical?"

Her father was listening from across the aisle, doing what he did best: hearing the abstract logic and figuring out how it applied to actual life. "You're saying the family is the anchor," he said. Not a question.

"I'm saying the family's love for each other is the anchor," Aaliyah corrected. "It's the only thing that works the same way in both timelines. It's what makes us choose to get on those flights. It's what makes us come back. It's what doesn't change even when time is breaking around us."

The morpho butterfly appeared, brilliant blue against the metro window. It didn't enter the car—it stayed pressed against the glass like it was checking that they were still okay. Like it was confirming their direction.

Abuela Ida's butterfly.

"She would have loved this," Elijah said thoughtfully. He was watching the butterfly with the focus of a child who'd decided that certain things in his life made sense in non-linear ways. "The most broken thing becoming the most clear thing. Turning the confusion into the answer."

The metro began to stabilize. Not into a single timeline—Aaliyah could still see the old 1920s route and the new route overlapping—but into a kind of harmony. Two systems that had learned to coexist. Two versions of navigation that had decided to travel together.

The announcement system crackled to life with an ARIA-7 transmission. Her voice was quieter than usual. Almost thoughtful.

"You are beginning to understand the architecture," the AI said. "The feedback loop is not what breaks you. The feedback loop is what teaches you. Continue toward Curaçao. Continue understanding. You are no longer in danger of erasure. You are becoming essential."

The transmission ended.

Luisana was writing in her journal, rapid shorthand, capturing the moment before the logic evaporated. Anthony was holding his phone down because the metro system had just taught him something about plans: sometimes the best ones were the ones you didn't know you were making.

Twenty-two days remained.

And for the first time, Aaliyah understood that they didn't have to solve time.

They just had to keep loving each other while it tried to solve itself around them.

The metro pulled into the next station.

Sully barked once, satisfied.

They were going exactly where they needed to be.
